date_or_window: 1 January 2027 -- calculated as one calendar year from the Decree-Law's own 1 January 2026 entry-into-force date; the CMA/Council may extend this period at its discretion, and no such extension has been located in this research pass. Article (83) ('Adjustment of Status'), fetched directly from the CMA's own domain, requires entities and individuals subject to the Decree-Law to be 'obligated to adjust their status in accordance with its provisions within one year' of its enforcement date, a period the Council may extend at its discretion; Article (85) separately provides that the Decree-Law was published in the Official Gazette and took effect on 1 January 2026.
event: Under the Capital Market Authority's (CMA) federal framework, applicable UAE-wide outside the financial free zones (DIFC and ADGM): Federal Decree-Law No. (33) of 2025 Concerning the Regulation of the Capital Market -- whose Article 39 brings virtual-asset trading and related financial activities/services within the CMA's regulatory perimeter, prohibiting trading in a virtual asset unless it is on the Authority's official virtual-asset list, traded via an Authority-approved licensed platform, and itself registered with the Authority -- requires all entities and individuals subject to its provisions (which, per Article 3(1), includes brokerage, portfolio management, financial advisory and custodian services, and per Article 39 extends to virtual-asset trading and related activities) to adjust their status in accordance with the Decree-Law's provisions within one year of its 1 January 2026 entry into force, i.e. by 1 January 2027, unless the Council extends the period. This is the Decree-Law's own general statutory transition mechanism; it is distinct from, and independently confirmed of, the separate transition/grace-period arrangements that multiple secondary legal-alert sources (Horizons & Co, Clyde & Co, Mielo Group, enterpriseam.com) report -- with mutually inconsistent dates (variously reported as 27 February 2027, 1 January 2027, and a 13 February 2027 module-specific deadline) -- for CMA Decision No. 4/R.M/2026 (the Virtual Assets Framework) specifically; this research could not independently fetch and confirm Decision No. 4/R.M/2026's own transitional-provisions text against the CMA's own domain this session (its regulations-listing and circulars pages are client-rendered search interfaces that this pipeline's fetch tooling could not resolve to underlying decision text), so that decision-specific grace period is not asserted here and is tracked only as an open item in this jurisdiction's pillar-state cards. Only the Decree-Law's own Article 83 deadline, directly fetched and quoted above, is asserted in this entry.

date_or_window: During 2026 -- VARA's own circular states only this calendar-year window, with no more specific date, for its assessment of implementation; the circular itself (dated 4 March 2026) took effect from its own date of issuance, but the assessment activity it describes is explicitly forward-looking and, as of this research (13 July 2026), roughly half of the stated window remains open
event: Under VARA's rules, applicable in the Emirate of Dubai (including its free zones, excluding the DIFC): a VARA circular to all Virtual Asset Service Providers (VASPs), dated 4 March 2026 and titled 'Implementation of the UAE Anti-Money Laundering, Counter-Terrorism Financing and Proliferation Financing Requirements Applicable to VASPs' -- issued following Cabinet Resolution No. (134) of 2025 (the Executive Regulations to the federal AML/CFT/CPF Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025, in force from 14 December 2025) -- sets out VARA's supervisory expectations for VASPs' alignment with the Executive Regulations (expanded risk-based framework including proliferation financing, customer due diligence thresholds, enhanced due diligence, virtual-asset-transfer/Travel Rule compliance, targeted-financial-sanctions controls, suspicious-transaction reporting, and record-keeping) and states that VARA will 'assess implementation of the Executive Regulations through off-site reviews, supervisory engagements, inspections and thematic reviews' during 2026, adding that VASPs failing to demonstrate effective implementation of the revised requirements may be required to undertake remedial action within specified timeframes and may be subject to supervisory or enforcement measures under VARA's regulatory mandate. This entry tracks only that stated 2026 supervisory-assessment window; it does not track the circular's own (already-elapsed, as of this research) 30/90/120-calendar-day gap-assessment deadlines from VARA's separate, earlier 24 November 2025 circular on the same underlying federal AML Decree-Law, which have already passed and are not forward-looking.
